observations.recommendation:
• The City Accountant prepare the necessary accounting entries for the
reclassification of the completed projects from CIP accounts to the
appropriate PPE accounts in coordination with the City Engineer,
supported by relevant reports/documents;
• The City Accountant record the corresponding depreciation expense,
including those pertaining to prior years as an adjustment to Government
Equity; and
• The City Engineer verify and make a report of the status of other projects,
for those which cannot be verified from accounting records, which
remained under the CIP account for several years, to facilitate the transfer
thereof to the appropriate PPE account.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to make the necessary
accounting entries to recognize the appropriate PPE account for the
completed project and settle the corresponding liability under the Due to
NGA account.

• The Inventory Committee include in the physical count the PPEs found at
station or items not included in both property and accounting records,
when there is reasonable basis to consider the same as owned by the City,
as required by COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
• The City Accountant takes up all the necessary accounting entries to
reconcile the GL balance against the SLs to determine the correct balance
of the City’s PPEs, provided that all the procedures on one-time cleansing
of PPE have been fully complied with.

• The City Accountant, the City Treasurer, and the City Assessor reconcile
their reports and records to establish the correct balance of RPT and SET
receivables and prepare the corresponding adjusting entries;
• The City Accountant carefully review the accuracy and completeness of
the accounting entries made for the collection of RPT and SET to ensure
that both accounts have the same balances; and
• The City Assessor and/or the City Treasurer regularly reconcile its
records with that of the City Accountant by submitting a report to the
latter any changes or correction to the initially recorded RPT collectibles.

• Secure/collate all available documents relative to the dormant accounts,
such as books of accounts, records, financial statements, ledgers, schedules,
vouchers/documents and all other documents;
• Review, analyze and determine the existence and validity of all dormant
receivables accounts and prepare the necessary schedules and documents
to be submitted to the Auditor, as prescribed in item 6 of COA Circular
No. 2023-008; and
• Until a new filing period is granted for the write-off of dormant receivables,
the Accountant must establish an allowance for impairment of these
receivables, in accordance with IPSAS 41.

• The City Accountant ensure that all purchases of supplies and materials
intended for stock/distribution are initially recorded in the appropriate
inventory accounts, in compliance with IPSAS No. 12 and the NGAS
Manual. Expenses should only be recognized upon issuance or actual
distribution to beneficiaries/recipients; and
• The General Services Office (GSO) or the concerned department in
custody of the goods for distribution maintain stock cards for all inventory
items and reconcile these regularly with the Accounting Office’s records,
to record utilization as expense.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to reconcile the loan
accounts per accounting records with the bank records and make the necessary
correcting or adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the Loans
Payable-Domestic Account in the financial statements.

The City Treasurer and the City Accountant reconcile their records under custody and make adjustments accordingly. Henceforth, conduct regular reconciliations to promptly resolve any differences between the cash book and general ledgers in order to present reliable cash in bank balances.

observations.recommendation:
1.13 We recommended and Management agreed that;
a. The GSO update the property cards (PCs) to include all unrecorded
acquisitions, issues, and disposal of PPE, if any;
b. The GSO prepares the list of PPEs found at the station and submits it
to the Accounting Unit for recording in the books;
c. The GSO makes a list of missing/non-existing PPEs, observes the
guidelines for the disposition of said properties, and completes all the
procedures prescribed in the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances enumerated under Item 6.3.1 (a to h) of COA Circular No.
2020-006;
d. The Accounting Unit takes up accounting entries to recognize PPE
found at the station prepares and maintains corresponding PPELCs
based on the list submitted by the GSO and adheres to all the
procedures stated in Item 6.3.2 (a to f) of COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020;
e. Ensure that Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs) are strictly
prepared for all acquired or procured properties. PARs are to be
utilized for documenting the receipt of property and equipment
intended for official use from the Designated Property Officer; and
f. The GSO and Accounting Unit reconcile their records and ensure that
the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the
PPE ledger cards (PPELCs) maintained by the Accounting Unit are
reconciled.

2.8 We recommended and the Accountant and GS Officer conformed to;
a. Reclassify the issued tangible items acquired prior to CY 2022 with
carrying amounts below the ₱50,000.00 threshold and expense the same to
Prior Period Adjustment and Government Equity, while tangible items
that are still in the custody of the Supply/Property Division shall be
recorded to the appropriate semi-expendable property accounts;
b. Likewise, the Accountant adjust the opening balance in the Statement of
Changes in Net Assets/Equity in compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-
004, dated May 31, 2022; and
c. The GSO maintains Semi-expendable Property Cards (SPCs) for semi-
expendable items under custody until these items are issued to end-users.

3.10 We recommended and the City Treasurer and the City Accountant agreed to
reconcile their records under custody and make adjustments accordingly.
Henceforth, conduct regular reconciliations to promptly resolve any
differences between the cash book and general ledgers in order to present
reliable cash in bank balances.

4.11 We recommended that the Mayor and the Accountant agreed to
a) Submit all cash advance vouchers for CF with the complete required
supporting document, within seven working days from the release of the
check to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) concerned in a sealed
envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL – For the COA ATL
Only” and with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the submissions
pursuant to Item 6.3.3.d COA, DILG, Governance Commission for
GOCCs, and DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015;
b) Ensure that supporting documents for the grant of cash advance are
complete and sufficient and in accordance with the itemized documents
under Item 6.1.7 and Item 6.1.8.3 of said Joint Circular; and
c) Secure a certification from the concerned PNP Chief relative to the peace
and order situation in the locality highlighting in concrete detail the
circumstances that require the urgency of allocating the CF and
supporting the need to release and use the CF.

5.9 We recommended and it was agreed that:
a. The City Development Council thoroughly evaluates the projects,
programs, and activities for inclusion in the 20% Development Fund Plan
and ensures that they are well-planned and procurement-and-
implementation-ready pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated
November 4, 2020, to meet the demands of the constituents; and
b. The Offices who are in charge of the monitoring of the utilization and
reporting of funds for the 20% Development Fund prepare and submit a
reconciled and accurate report to the concerned users for effective and
efficient carry-through of the planned PPAs.

6.8 We recommended and it was agreed that:
a. The City Health Office submits proof of eligibility of the listed recipients
of the HEA; and
b. The City Treasurer’s office requires a photocopy of any proof of
identification (ID) with specimen signatures of the recipients for easy
verification.

7.11 We recommended and Management agreed to promptly submit the
LDRRMFIP to ensure that the program, projects, and activities related to
disaster risk reduction and management activities charged against the
LDRRMF are incorporated in the said plan as the basis for the release and
use of the fund pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated
September 12, 2012.

1. The City Accountant shall require the officials and employees of the City and National Government Agencies to liquidate their cash advances immediately thru the issuance of demand letters.
2. The Management shall strictly observe the rules and regulations in the liquidation of cash advances pursuant to Item 5.1 of COA Circular no. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; otherwise, impose sanctions by withholding their salaries according to the stated rules and regulations.
3. A validation and evaluation of the cash advances shall be done to determine the collectibility of the accounts and request for authority to write-off in accordance with the provision of COA Circular no. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters have been sent out for the liquidation of cash advances.

There were cash advances which have been granted to personnel of national offices and barangay officials. These personnels are no longer connected with the agency.
Validation in progress, until cash advances at year end will show a lower balance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to constantly monitor the RPT/SET receivables on real properties and address the need to intensify collection of the delinquent taxpayers. Receivable account in order to properly record the current year 20% and the 30% Barangay Share.
We further recommended the City Treasurer constantly monitor the RPT/SET Receivables on Real Property Taxes. Monitor the re-assesssment of City Assessor's in accordance with the law.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
a. Evaluate unpaid obligations totaling ₱3.453 million that remained outstanding for two years or more. If no actual claim, administrative or judicial, has been filed or which is not covered by perfected contracts or records, he may recommend for the reversion of the same pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445;
b. Process invoices/AP on time, and track payments; and
c. Regularly check, monitor, and keep the Accounts Payable account periodically updated.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Return all unused/unutilized balances of completed projects to the respective Source Agencies in compliance with the conditions in the MOA and Item 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994; and
b. Remit all collections under the Due to NGAs account to the concerned National Government Agencies immediately after the receipt of the fees and reclassify/prepare adjustments for misclassified accounts included in the Due to NGAs account.

We recommended and Management agreed to draw journal entries to reclassify the total liquidated damages from liability to PPE accounts by debiting Due to Officers and Employees and crediting the affected PPE account and, henceforth, observe the proper accounting treatment of liquidated damages following the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended and the City Accounting Officer agreed to furnish the Accounting Office with the Receipt of Inventory Stock of vaccines from the Province. We also recommended that the City Accountant recognize in the books the remaining unutilized vaccines as donations in kind based on the market or appraise value pursuant to the requirement of IPSAS No 23 or obtain the prices of vaccines from the Provincial Health Office, Negros Occidental.
We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to ensure that the preparation of the BRS is given priority in consonance with the stated regulation.

We recommended and the LGU agreed to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund considering that these are priority projects for the immediate needs of the constituents for socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City pursuant to the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Motor pool personnel in charge in the issuance of fuel intended for the two access road projects to support the issuance of fuels for heavy equipment with Trip Tickets and Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS). We further recommended the submission of a Summary of Gasoline/Fuel released for the usage of heavy equipment, subject to these purchases.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare and submit bank reconciliation statements for all accounts with the required supporting schedules and documents to COA within the prescribed period to allow timely verification thereof. We further recommended that the personnel in charge at the Accounting Office shall record/adjust reconciling items so that the accuracy and reliability of the Cash-in-Bank account can be validated.
observations.reason: Submitted documents on file in COA, Himamaylan City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant adopt measures to require liquidation of outstanding fund transfers through verification of the existence of the recipient NGOs/POs, verification of project implementation and sending demand letters. Otherwise, demand return of transferred funds if utilization is not in accordance with the MOA. We further recommended that the City Mayor refrain from granting additional fund transfers to NGOs/POs with previous unsettled balances and impose strict implementation of the pertinent provisions of the grants.

We recommended that the City Accountant ensure that the personnel in charge at the Accounting Office initially record purchases of medical, dental, and laboratory supplies as inventory. Personnel in charge at GSO and Accounting Office maintain RIS and ledger cards, respectively, and coordinate the conduct of the physical count of undistributed supplies or goods at the end of the year for recognition of appropriate asset and expense accounts at year-end.

Medical, dental and laboratory supplies are being recognized in the books as outright expense which was already covered by an RIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWD Officer furnish the City Accounting Office with a detailed list of donations in-kind received by the City, and the City Accountant recognize in the books the donation in-kind in accordance with existing regulations. We further recommended the installation of a system in relation to accounting for receipts and disposition of donations in-kind.

a. We recommended that the City Health Officer furnish the City Accountant with a detailed list of allocated vaccines received for proper recording in the books of accounts.
b. For donations received with costs, we recommended that the City Accountant record these in the books in accordance with existing regulations and recognize donations received without costs at their fair value as at date of receipt, which may be ascertained by reference to an active market, or by appraisal as provided in Paragraph 97 of the IPSAS No. 23.

We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with the national government agency in charge with the vaccination program to immediately issue clear guidelines as to the proper disposal of expired vaccines if extension of emergency use authority (EUA) cannot be warranted. We also recommended that the City Government through the National Immunization Program Coordinator, prioritize and expedite the use of about to expire vaccines to prevent wastage, and formulate internal control policies necessary to minimize the occurrence of expired/damaged vaccines.

We recommended that the City shall consider the immediate disposal of unserviceable and obsolete properties and take up any proceeds from the sale thereof in compliance with Section 79 of PD 1445, after which they shall prepare journal entries to drop from the books of accounts.
observations.reason: The publication for public auction for properties determined to be unservicable but found with salvage value has been conducted. However, there was no interested buyer came the date set for the purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Government implement the closure of the open dumpsite as mandated under Section 41 of RA 9003 and to operationalize the sanitary landfill facility so that solid waste management will be properly managed, controlled, and contained to ensure the protection of public health and preservation of environment can be achieved.
observations.reason: The sanitary landfill is currently operational and all its facilities are functional.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant prepare and submit bank reconciliation statements of all accounts with the required supporting schedules and documents to COA within the prescribed period to allow timely verification thereof.
We further recommended that the personnel in charge at the accounting office record/adjust reconciling items so that the accuracy and reliability of the Cash-in-Bank account can be validated.

We recommended that the City Accountant require officials and employees to settle immediately unliquidated cash advances, especially those which have remained outstanding for more than 1 year, otherwise require the persons liable to refund the amount advanced to avoid the institution of any charges against them.

We recommended that the City Accountant and General Services Officer complete the required reconciliation of property records and effect the necessary adjustments to fairly present pertinent accounts in the financial statement.
Furthermore, exert effort and investigate the noted reconciling differences and if found that these properties are missing, hold the accountable officer liable thereof.
We also recommended that the General Services Officer determine ownership of all unrecorded property and equipment found during the physical count, prepare/issue Property Acknowledgement Receipt to the end-user of the item and record the same in the PEC and RPCPPE, taking into account the provisions under COA Circular No. 2020-006 on one-time cleansing of PPE accounts. Include a separate caption of the unrecorded property and equipment found at station in the RPCPPE which shall be the basis of the Accounting Office in recording the subject property and equipment in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the City Accountant adopt measures to require liquidation of outstanding fund transfers through verification of the existence of the recipient NGOs/POs, verification of project implementation and sending demand letters. Otherwise, demand return of transferred funds if utilization is not in accordance with the MOA.
We further recommended that the City Mayor refrain from granting additional fund transfers to NGOs/POs with previous unsettled balances and impose strict implementation of the pertinent provisions of the grants.

We recommended that the City Accountant ensure that the personnel in charge at the Accounting Office initially record purchases of medical, dental and laboratory supplies as inventory. Further, personnel in charge at the GSO and Accounting Office maintain RIS and ledger cards, respectively, and coordinate for the conduct of physical count of undistributed supplies or goods at the end of the year for recognition of appropriate asset and expense accounts at year-end.
We recommended that the City Treasurer constantly monitor the receivables from RPT/SET on real properties and intensify collection of the delinquent accounts of taxpayers.

We recommended that the CSWD Officer furnish the City Accounting Office with a detailed list of donations in-kind received by the City, and the City Accountant recognize in the books the donation in-kind in accordance with existing regulations.
We further recommended installation of a system in relation to accounting for receipts and disposition of donations in-kind.

We recommended that the City Administrator require the submission of necessary documents to support monetization of leave credits for more than 50% of the available leave balance; and henceforth, implement strictly the provisions of Sec. 22 and 23 of the CSC Omnibus Rules on Leave and COA Circular N0o. 2012-001.

We recommended that the required supporting documents in granting and liquidation of cash advances be submitted to ascertain the accuracy, completeness and propriety of the COVID-19 Special Risk Allowance in accordance to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the City Health Officer furnish the City Accountant with a detailed list of allocated vaccines received for proper recording in the books of accounts.
For donations received with costs, we recommended that the City Accountant record these in the books in accordance with existing regulations and recognize donations received without costs at their fair value as at date of receipt, which may be ascertained by reference to an active market, or by appraisal as provided in Paragraph 97 of the IPSAS No. 23.
We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with the national government agency in charge with the vaccination program to immediately issue clear guidelines as to the proper disposal of expired vaccines, if extension of emergency use authority (EUA) cannot be warranted.
We also recommended that the City Government through the National Immunization Program Coordinator, prioritize and expedite the use of about to expire vaccines to prevent wastage, and formulate internal control policies necessary to minimize the occurrence of expired/damaged vaccines.

We recommended that the Accountant promptly submit the required monthly and annual Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the Audit Team, in accordance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person identify gender issues in accordance with the guidelines prescribed by PCW-DILGDBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and the amendments set by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 and ensure that the GAD Fund allocation shall be utilized for PPAs to promote women empowerment and gender equality.

We recommended that the accounting office reconcile the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements with the balances in the trial balance and record/adjust necessary reconciling items so that the accuracy and reliability of the Cash-in-Bank account can be validated.

We recommended that the Accounting Office and GSO complete the required reconciliation of property records pursuant to Section 114 of Volume I of the NGAS Manual and effect the necessary adjustments to fairly present the said accounts in the financial statement. Furthermore, the Accounting Office and GSO investigate the noted differences and if found that these properties are missing, hold the accountable officer liable thereof. We also recommended that the GSO determine ownership of all unrecorded property and equipment found during the physical count, prepare/issue Property Acknowledgement Receipt to the end-user of the item and record the same in the PEC and RPCPPE. The RPCPPE should include a separate caption of the unrecorded property and equipment found in the station which shall be the basis of the Accounting Office in recording the subject property and equipment in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of data and records between Accounting and GSO offices is still on-going.

We recommended that the City consider the immediate disposal of unserviceable and obsolete properties and take up any proceeds from the sale thereof in compliance with Section 79 of P.D. 1445, after which they shall prepare journal entries to drop from the books of accounts.
observations.reason: The publication for public auction for properties determined to be unserviceable but found with salvage value has been conducted. However, there was no interested buyer came the date set for the purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City maximize the GAD Fund by fully implementing programs, projects and activities in accordance with the guidelines set forth in the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 as amended by JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.

We recommended that the Management thru the CSWD, LDRRM, and CHO Offices, require that the original copies of the distribution lists of goods and supplies or list of recipients identified barangay or quarantine facility as a basis for confirmation and validation of the funds disbursed. We also recommended that the Accounting Office ensure that the required itemized list of goods distributed to recipients/beneficiaries is attached to the DVs for ease of verification.

We recommended that the BAC update or revise their APP and post it on the GPPB website as required by the GPPB Circular. We further recommended that the BAC or the Accounting Office furnish the Audit Team copies of the lacking documentary requirements to ensure that the accredited suppliers strictly comply with the requirements of the GPPB Guidelines for Emergency Procurement.

We recommended that: Management requires the BAC to disseminate widely the Notice of Bid among qualified suppliers/contractors to obtain competitive prices; and the BAC periodically reviews the history of bidders for particular procurement/projects and discern suspicious patterns of bid collusion among suppliers/contractors and impose sanctions thereto if warranted.

We recommended that the City Engineers Office submit regularly to the Office of the City Accountant the Accomplishment Report on Infrastructure Projects and copies of Certificate of Turnover to be used as basis for the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the transfer to appropriate PPE – Public Infrastructure accounts. We further recommended that the City Accountant adhere to the provisions of Section 50 of the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I to enable the close monitoring of the projects still in progress and to make necessary adjusting entries to transfer the identified completed projects to fairly present the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant require officials and employees to liquidate and/or refund immediately unliquidated cash advances, especially those remained outstanding for more than 1 year to five years, otherwise, refund the amount advanced to avoid the institution of administrative and criminal charges against the erring officials.
